transcript.transcript: Hi, I'm Tom Brown, uh, volunteering with the Emmet Orchards Oral history Project. Uh, today is Monday, March 2nd, 2026. I'm in Beaverton, Oregon, in the home of my second cousin, Mark Wills and his wife, Joan, here in Beaverton. And today, um, we'll talk about Mark's early life in Emmett, uh, and, um, his experience in family orchards there. This oral history is conducted with the support of the Idaho Historical Society as part of Idaho's commemoration of America. 250 so, Mark, uh, I, I want to thank you for welcoming me into your home and for your time today. Um, this interview will allow you to reflect on our heritage and opportunity to share your experiences and memories that can be preserved. He seemed really happy to get us together. He was curious about what we were playing. We played pirates in the garage, okay? And he went in the house to visit with mom while we played, right. I thought I was going to witness a murder. She did not like that response. She didn't know she did. Well, I mean, like I said, she took great pride. I mean, you know, we talked about that when on the, um, on the, um, when we were talking about we could tell in the packing house, you know, which orchards were being processed at a given time. You know, from the from the prospect of the of the box that had all the, the, the undersized cherries, you know, some of them, they'd be coming through like crazy, right. Other ones. No. They'd all go under the ones that were going to get the big money and. Hers, hers were the big cherries. Absolutely. All right, all right. So intense pride. Yes. Oh, yes. Yes. Yeah. Well, Mark, it's been delightful to spend this time with you and hear your memories and stories and the impact of our heritage, um, on your life. It's wonderful to share that with you. I, I appreciate the time to get to talk to you. And, uh, hope we have other chances to talk, even if it's off the record. For sure. For sure. Thank you so much, Mark. Thanks, Tom. All right.

story.title: narrator Mark Wills by TBROWN 2026-03-02
story.description: The oral history interview with Mark Wills provides a vivid recollection of life and work in family orchards in Emmett, Idaho. Conducted as part of the Emmet Orchards Oral History Project by Tom Brown, this recording delves into Mark's childhood memories of working in the orchards, his grandfather Frank Wills' influence, and the family's connections in the fruit packing industry, notably with the Gem Fruit Union. The discussion highlights the family's enduring work ethic, their pride in maintaining high-quality produce, and their impact on Mark's career in technology. Additionally, there is a sentimental exploration of family heirlooms and the role Mark's grandmother, Cecelia, played in family and community life.
